learning_sequence_context.position_in_sequence: Lesson 3 of 10 in the Term 1-2 sequence - Lesson 1 of the 8-lesson PowerPoint unit, and the start of the 'About me' presentation golden thread.
learning_sequence_context.previous_lesson_summary: Last lesson pupils completed the NGRT reading assessment; before that, in Lesson 1, they learned lab rules and basic digital skills (OneNote, OneDrive, file handling).
learning_sequence_context.next_lesson_link: Next lesson pupils transfer their mind map into PowerPoint's outline view, beginning the 'About me' presentation itself.

expected_outcomes.lesson_objective: Pupils can describe what a mind map is, why it is used, and how it is used in planning, and can create a mind map about themselves with a central theme, at least three main branches, and at least two levels of sub-branching.
assessment_criteria: Each student will create a central node clearly labelled as themselves ('About Me').
Each student will add at least three main branches (e.g. favourite subject, food, movie, hobby).
Each student will add sub-branches beneath at least two main branches, aiming for the highest level of structure they can reach (Structural to Extended).
Each student will identify and label the constituent parts of a mind map (central theme, nodes, sub-nodes) when asked.
expected_outcomes.activity_summary: Individual construction of an 'About Me' mind map recording favourite subject, food, movie, things to watch, a hobby, favourite thing at school, and a recent achievement, built up through four increasingly detailed structural levels.
expected_outcomes.cross_curricular_or_real_world_link: Mind maps are a general-purpose planning tool used across subjects (e.g. planning an essay in English, revising a topic in Science) and in life beyond school (e.g. planning a project or an event).

resources: Worked example mind map (printed or on-screen)

key_vocabulary.definition: A diagram used to visually organise information around a central theme, with branching nodes and sub-nodes.
A single idea or point on a mind map, connected by a branch to the central theme or to another node.
An ordering of ideas from general (central) to specific (sub-branches).

preparation.additional_adult_role: Support pupils who default to a linear list rather than a branching structure, using the worked example to prompt re-grouping into main branches and sub-branches.
accessibility: Printed worked example as an alternative to the on-screen model
Sentence starters provided for pupils who need support generating personal facts

teaching_episode.teacher_actions: Display three quick-fire questions recapping Lesson 1: lab rules, opening OneNote, opening File Explorer to the documents folder.

check_for_understanding.question: Where do you go to find your saved files?
check_for_understanding.decision_rule: Proceed if most pupils correctly name File Explorer/documents; otherwise briefly re-demonstrate.

teaching_episode.teacher_actions: Explain what a mind map is, why it is used, and how it supports planning; introduce the constituent parts (central theme, nodes, sub-nodes) using a worked example unrelated to 'About Me'.
teaching_episode.pupil_actions: Watch the worked example and identify the central theme, a node, and a sub-node when asked.

check_for_understanding.question: In this example, which part is the central theme and which is a node?
check_for_understanding.decision_rule: Move to modelling regardless; use responses to judge how much further explanation of hierarchy is needed.

teaching_episode.teacher_actions: Model live construction of a central node and three main branches in the mind-mapping software for a sample 'favourite things' topic, then add a second level of sub-branches, thinking aloud about how each idea relates to its parent.
teaching_episode.pupil_actions: Watch and note the difference between adding a new main branch and adding a sub-branch of an existing one.

teaching_episode.teacher_actions: Set the 'About Me' mind map task, explaining the four differentiated structure levels (Structural, Multi-structural, Relational, Extended); circulate offering least-help-first prompts to pupils stalled at a linear list.
teaching_episode.pupil_actions: Individually record personal facts (favourite subject, food, movie, things to watch, a hobby, favourite thing at school, a recent achievement) and build their 'About Me' mind map, working up through the four levels as time allows.

check_for_understanding.question: Does your mind map have at least two branches with a sub-branch beneath them yet?
check_for_understanding.decision_rule: Pupils still on a single-level list at the 15-minute mark receive a targeted prompt referencing the worked example.

teaching_episode.teacher_actions: Facilitate a paired swap-and-check against the four structure levels; cold-call two pairs to share which level they reached and one branch they are proud of.
teaching_episode.pupil_actions: Swap with a partner, identify which structural level the partner's mind map has reached, and give one piece of specific feedback.

check_for_understanding.question: Exit ticket: which structural level did your mind map reach, and what was your central theme?
check_for_understanding.decision_rule: Pupils reporting only Level 1 are flagged for a brief recap at the start of next lesson before starting the PowerPoint outline.
